abrupt_song_end_no_fade.applicability_text: Album tracks (not radio singles); songs that want to leave listener unsettled; tracks where resolution would feel cheap.
abrupt_song_end_no_fade.trade_offs: Won't suit radio-singles needing satisfying close. Risk of feeling like a mistake to listeners conditioned to fades.
abrupt_song_end_no_fade.documented_example: Territorial Pissings ends abruptly mid-phrase — no fade, no resolution. Hard cut.

wallace_compressed_brightness_vs_albini_.rule: When mixing aggressive material (fast tempo, max distortion, screamed vocal), apply Wallace-style heavy compression + bright EQ rather than Albini-style raw room emphasis. The compressed brightness translates intensity to small speakers + commercial playback contexts. Album-level mix philosophy choice.
wallace_compressed_brightness_vs_albini_.electronic_translation: For aggressive electronic tracks targeting club/Spotify, apply HEAVY MIX-BUS COMPRESSION (Vari-Mu or SSL-style 4:1 with 2-3dB GR) + BRIGHTNESS EQ (high-shelf +2-3dB at 8kHz, master-bus tilt) + master limiter to -7 to -8 LUFS. The compressed brightness translates the aggression to small speakers (phones, laptops, club tops). Bright signal cuts through. Apply for: industrial-techno radio/streaming masters, EBM commercial releases, dark-electronic peak-time tracks, gabber/hard-techno club masters. AVOID for ambient (kills dynamic detail), neo-classical-electronic (incompatible with classical-tradition warmth), dub-techno (kills spatial decay).
wallace_compressed_brightness_vs_albini_.applicability_text: Aggressive drops where mix clarity matters; tracks where the listener needs to perceive structure within max density; anything mastering for radio-target loudness.
wallace_compressed_brightness_vs_albini_.trade_offs: Sacrifices dynamic detail for radio-impact. Risk of fatigue if compression too heavy. Won't suit intimate or atmospheric material.
wallace_compressed_brightness_vs_albini_.documented_example: Andy Wallace's mix of Territorial Pissings + Nevermind — compressed bright aesthetic delivering aggressive material to small-speaker contexts.

screamed_single_take_vocal_no_doubling.rule: Track the vocal as SINGLE-TAKE screamed delivery. No doubling, no harmony, no comping from many short pieces. The PERFORMANCE INTEGRITY is preserved — listener hears ONE moment of vocal commitment rather than a constructed-from-fragments illusion.
screamed_single_take_vocal_no_doubling.electronic_translation: Track aggressive vocal in ONE COMPLETE TAKE — no comping syllables from multiple takes, no doubling, no harmony layers. Heavy saturation (Decapitator on full vocal, or SansAmp-style amp-modeling for grit) preserved as DELIVERY ARTIFACT. The performance integrity matters: the listener hears one moment of vocal commitment. Apply for: industrial-techno screamed vocals (one-shot Reznor-style aggression), EBM front-man delivery, dark-electronic peak-vocal moments, glitch-corruption single-take captures. Pair with vocal_saturation_for_hardcore_punk_aesthetic processing chain. The recording session: single 2-min take, mic at 4-6 inches with high-pass-filter on vocal chain, comp settings already locked (don't track-then-process — process while tracking so vocalist hears the saturation in ear-mix).
screamed_single_take_vocal_no_doubling.applicability_text: Aggressive tracks where physical performance authenticity is the goal; vocal moments that should feel like ONE moment, not a layered moment.
screamed_single_take_vocal_no_doubling.trade_offs: Demands vocalist with screaming capacity + ability to deliver in single take. Risk of vocal performance not surviving multiple-track-take comping safety net.
screamed_single_take_vocal_no_doubling.documented_example: Territorial Pissings vocal — Cobain single-take screamed delivery, no doubling, the performance IS the recording.

descending_riff_as_song_identity.rule: Build the entire song around a single DESCENDING power chord (or pitched motif) pattern. The descent should move in steps or thirds and complete within 1-2 bars. The riff IS the song — verses, choruses, bridge all variations on this single descending shape.
descending_riff_as_song_identity.electronic_translation: Build entire track around DESCENDING SYNTH MOTIF — bass-line or lead synth phrase that descends step-wise or in thirds across 1-2 bars (e.g., A → G → F → E within Aeolian mode). The descent loops continuously with micro-variations (octave shifts, rhythmic displacement, filter modulation). Apply on a Massive / Diva / Sub 37 with strong character (square + saw stack, pulse-width modulation, slight FM, drive). Apply for: industrial-techno (descending bass-lines as track-identity — Surgeon, Perc tradition), dark-electronic riff-driven tracks, IDM motif-obsession compositions, EBM signature bass-lines. The descending shape IS the song — verses, breakdowns, drops all variations on this single shape, never escaping its gravitational pull.
descending_riff_as_song_identity.applicability_text: Riff-centered tracks where the riff IS the song; compositions where harmonic motion would compete with rhythmic identity; drop-tuned aggressive material.
descending_riff_as_song_identity.trade_offs: Demands compelling riff — single shape carries full song. Risk of monotony if riff lacks identity.
descending_riff_as_song_identity.documented_example: Very Ape — descending power-chord pattern as the song's complete compositional content.

mid_tempo_aggression_not_hardcore_speed.rule: Aggression does not require fast tempo. At 100-115 BPM with full distortion + locked rhythm + strained vocal, the song achieves max aggressive identity while remaining headbangable rather than hardcore-fast. Mid-tempo aggression as compositional alternative to punk-speed.
mid_tempo_aggression_not_hardcore_speed.electronic_translation: Resist the techno-default 130-140 BPM imperative. Lock tempo at 100-115 BPM with FULL DENSITY (heavy bass synth, saturated kick, distorted percussive layers) — the slower tempo lets each kick/snare LAND with weight rather than blur. The aggression comes from density + saturation + harmonic friction, not velocity. Apply for: industrial-techno head-nod tracks (Perc, Ancient Methods slower side), dark-electronic mid-tempo grit, EBM signature tempo (Front 242 'Headhunter' lineage, ~106 BPM aggressive identity), trip-hop-meets-industrial fusion. Pair with `descending_riff_as_song_identity` for synth-bass-driven mid-tempo aggression. AVOID for hardcore-techno (genre demands 150+ BPM), gabber, peak-time festival drops.
mid_tempo_aggression_not_hardcore_speed.applicability_text: Aggressive sections where headbang feel matters more than speed; tracks targeting QOTSA-style measured aggression; sections that pair with slow burns elsewhere on the album.
mid_tempo_aggression_not_hardcore_speed.trade_offs: Less obvious 'aggression' for listeners conditioned to punk-speed. Demands strong riff/vocal identity to compensate for absence of velocity.
mid_tempo_aggression_not_hardcore_speed.documented_example: Very Ape at ~108 BPM — aggressive identity via density + vocal strain rather than via fast tempo.

compact_aggressive_under_2_minutes.rule: Aggressive tracks should resist the urge to extend. Restrict total runtime to under 2 minutes. The brevity prevents the relentless intensity from becoming monotonous; listener leaves wanting another hit rather than fatigued.
compact_aggressive_under_2_minutes.electronic_translation: Lock arrangement-view length at 1:30-1:55 for aggressive electronic tracks. NO breakdown, NO 32-bar buildup, NO extended outro. Cold-start intro → locked aggression → abrupt end (or 4-bar outro tail). The brevity prevents listener fatigue at peak density. Apply for: industrial-techno album-tracks (palette punctuation between 6-8 min epics), EBM punk-energy bursts, dark-electronic short statements, glitch/IDM compact violence (Aphex Twin 'Come To Daddy' EP-track economy ~2 min). The track ends BEFORE listener exhaustion — delete the temptation to extend. AVOID for ambient (genre incompatible with brevity), neo-classical-electronic (form demands development), peak-time DJ-tools (need 6+ min for mixing). Pair with `unrelenting_aggression_no_dynamic_arc` and `abrupt_song_end_no_fade` for tightest compressed-aggression statement.
compact_aggressive_under_2_minutes.applicability_text: Album tracks of aggressive nature (not radio singles); tracks meant as 'punctuation' between longer songs; sections where the same intensity over 4 minutes would feel exhausting.
compact_aggressive_under_2_minutes.trade_offs: Limits compositional ambition. Won't suit material requiring development.
compact_aggressive_under_2_minutes.documented_example: Very Ape at ~1:55 — aggressive intensity contained within compressed runtime, listener leaves wanting more.

guitar_alone_intro_4_bars_no_a_cappella.rule: Open the song with the GUITAR ALONE for 4 bars (riff established without other instruments). Distinct from HSB's similar approach because Very Ape's intro is at AGGRESSIVE INTENSITY rather than restrained — guitar enters at full distortion + tempo from bar 1. Riff identity established before band lock-in.
guitar_alone_intro_4_bars_no_a_cappella.electronic_translation: Open track with LEAD SYNTH OR BASS-RIFF ALONE for 4 bars at full intensity (full distortion, full saturation, no fade-in, no filter sweep). The motif is established BEFORE drums/bass arrive — riff IS the introduction. Different from cold-drop intros: the synth enters at full character from bar 1, not as a slowly revealing layer. Apply on Massive / Sub 37 / Diva with character-first patches (saw stack + drive + chorus). Apply for: industrial-techno riff-driven intros (the synth bass-line starts naked, kick joins at bar 5), dark-electronic motif-establishment intros, EBM signature-riff openers (the riff IS the song's calling card), IDM aggressive riff-led pieces. The 4-bar establishment teaches the listener the motif before adding rhythmic weight. Pair with `descending_riff_as_song_identity` for synth-bass-led aggressive intros, OR `delayed_rhythm_section_entry_4_bars` for paced full-band entry.
guitar_alone_intro_4_bars_no_a_cappella.applicability_text: Aggressive tracks needing immediate identity; songs where the riff IS the introduction; alternative to a-cappella fakeout for songs where vocal isn't the centerpiece.
guitar_alone_intro_4_bars_no_a_cappella.trade_offs: Demands compelling riff to hold attention solo. Less surprise than a-cappella variant.
guitar_alone_intro_4_bars_no_a_cappella.documented_example: Very Ape opens with descending riff alone for 4 bars at full distortion before band enters.

albini_mix_vs_wallace_mix_for_aggression.rule: When mixing aggressive material, two valid philosophies: ALBINI (Very Ape, Dumb-style) preserves room ambience and dynamic range — the aggression has SPACE to land. WALLACE (Nevermind-style) compresses + brightens — the aggression has FORCE to punch. Choose per album/track artistic intent rather than defaulting to one.
albini_mix_vs_wallace_mix_for_aggression.electronic_translation: Two valid mix philosophies for aggressive electronic tracks. RAW-CAPTURE (Albini analog): minimal mix-bus comp, preserved transient peaks (DR 9-11), spatial reverb tails kept long, distortion as instrument-level character not master-bus glue. The aggression has SPACE — dub-techno-adjacent, lo-fi industrial, rough-cut techno. COMMERCIAL-FORCE (Wallace digital): heavy mix-bus glue (Vari-Mu 3-4dB GR), brightness shelf at 8kHz, master limiter to -7 LUFS, transients clipped flat. The aggression has FORCE — peak-time techno, EBM commercial releases, festival-targeting dark-electronic. Choose per track artistic intent rather than defaulting to loudness-war commercial polish. Apply: Pole / Basic Channel / Vatican Shadow track lineage = raw-capture; Perc Trax / Sandwell District commercial pressings = commercial-force.
albini_mix_vs_wallace_mix_for_aggression.applicability_text: Mix decisions on aggressive tracks; selecting between live-document and commercial-target sound; aligning mix philosophy with composition intent.
albini_mix_vs_wallace_mix_for_aggression.trade_offs: Demands aesthetic clarity per track. Producing alternate mixes doubles cost.
albini_mix_vs_wallace_mix_for_aggression.documented_example: In Utero (Albini) vs. Nevermind (Wallace) — same band's aggressive material mixed via opposite philosophies, both valid.

outro_chant_extension_via_repetition_not.rule: End the song via a single PHRASE (vocal, instrumental, or both) repeated for an extended duration (~1:00+ minutes). Repetition is the build mechanism — vocal layers stack, instruments build density, but the underlying phrase stays static. Listener leaves the song hypnotized rather than satisfied. Album-closer-grade ending.
outro_chant_extension_via_repetition_not.electronic_translation: Vocoded or pitched-down vocal phrase (or arpeggiated synth motif) looped for 60-90+ seconds at song's outro. Build over the loop via PROGRESSIVE LAYER ADDITION not via volume: at start of outro, single phrase + minimal pad. Every 8 bars add another layer (second vocoded harmony at 5th, atmospheric pad in upper register, granular-shimmer above, tape-saturation drive on bus, side-chain pump intensifies, reverb send increases). Stop adding 16 bars before end + start STRIPPING layers in reverse — leaves listener with single phrase + reverb tail. NO FADE-OUT — final phrase ends abruptly OR resolves into single sustained reverb. Apply for: techno breakdown-resolutions, deep-house outros, ambient album-closers, downtempo emotional closers, dub-techno extended endings. Daft Punk / Caribou / Floating Points tradition.
outro_chant_extension_via_repetition_not.applicability_text: Album closers needing resolution function; songs wanting meditative weight as the ending; tracks where listener should leave the song hypnotized rather than satisfied.
outro_chant_extension_via_repetition_not.trade_offs: Demands material strong enough to bear minute-long repetition. Risk of feeling indulgent if phrase weak. Adds significant runtime — not for compressed-economy songs.
outro_chant_extension_via_repetition_not.documented_example: All Apologies — closing 'all in all is all we are' chant repeated ~1+ minute as the album's hypnotic resolution.

vocal_layering_as_chant_build_mechanism.rule: Build a vocal section's intensity via PROGRESSIVE LAYERING (single → doubled → stacked) rather than via volume or vocal performance escalation. Each successive iteration adds ONE more vocal layer. The 'build' is layer count, not loudness. Particularly suited to extended chant outros.
vocal_layering_as_chant_build_mechanism.electronic_translation: Build outro/breakdown intensity via PROGRESSIVE VOCAL LAYER ADDITION rather than via loudness or filter-opening. Iteration 1 = single dry vocal. Iteration 2 = + octave-doubled layer (pitched +1oct via pitch-shifter or take). Iteration 3 = + harmonized third above. Iteration 4 = + vocoder pad re-singing same line through Reaktor / Output Portal. Iteration 5 = + reverb-drenched whisper layer. Each repeat adds ONE element. Apply for: trip-hop outro builds (Massive Attack / Portishead chant outros), electronic-pop chorus intensification (Daft Punk 'Around the World'-style accumulation), neo-classical-electronic vocal-stacking (Nils Frahm / Olafur Arnalds choral builds), ambient slow-build vocal pieces. Pair with `outro_chant_extension_via_repetition_not_fade` for extended hypnotic build. The technical setup: route each layer to its own bus + reverb send so blend can be automated independently across the build.
vocal_layering_as_chant_build_mechanism.applicability_text: Outro chants; extended chorus repetitions; any moment where build is wanted but volume cannot increase further.
vocal_layering_as_chant_build_mechanism.trade_offs: Requires multi-take vocal capacity + careful blend in mix. Risk of layered chorus sounding artificial if takes too tightly aligned.
vocal_layering_as_chant_build_mechanism.documented_example: All Apologies outro — vocal layers stacked progressively across the chant repetition, build via density not volume.

cello_dual_role_pad_plus_melodic_respons.rule: Use cello (or bowed string) for BOTH harmonic pad function AND occasional melodic counterpoint to vocal phrases. The cello sustains chord tones throughout (pad function) AND occasionally answers vocal lines with brief melodic figures (response function). Single instrument doing two compositional jobs.
cello_dual_role_pad_plus_melodic_respons.electronic_translation: Use a SINGLE BOWED-STRING SAMPLER LIBRARY (Spitfire LABS Cello, Spitfire BBCSO cello sustains, Orchestral Tools cello, Embertone Blakus solo cello) or live-tracked cello for DUAL ROLE within an electronic arrangement: (1) sustained harmonic pad — chord tones held throughout the section as background warmth; (2) occasional melodic response figures — short 1-2 bar phrases answering the vocal/lead synth in the gaps between phrases. Single instrument, two compositional jobs. Apply for: neo-classical-electronic compositions (Nils Frahm / Olafur Arnalds / Hauschka tradition — single cello as compositional partner to piano/synth), trip-hop ballad arrangements (Portishead 'Glory Box'-adjacent), ambient-with-melody pieces, electronic-pop emotionally weighted closers. The arrangement discipline: write the pad part FIRST as a 4-chord loop, then write 3-4 short melodic-response figures that fit BETWEEN vocal phrases (not over them). Light convolution reverb (small chamber, 1.2s) to glue the cello into the electronic stems.
cello_dual_role_pad_plus_melodic_respons.applicability_text: Vocal-forward songs wanting orchestral depth; ballad arrangements wanting movement underneath the vocal; anywhere a string section would feel too thick but cello-alone provides the right weight.
cello_dual_role_pad_plus_melodic_respons.trade_offs: Requires available cellist with arranging sensibility. Risk of competing with vocal if response figures too prominent.
cello_dual_role_pad_plus_melodic_respons.documented_example: All Apologies cello part by Kera Schaley — sustained pad underneath + occasional melodic response between vocal phrases.

album_closer_resolution_function.rule: Position a track as deliberate album CLOSER — its structural function is resolution of the album, not just resolution of the song. Compositional and arrangement decisions serve the closing function: extended outro, hypnotic chant, fade or sustained ending. The track's role extends BEYOND its runtime to inform the album's interpretation.
album_closer_resolution_function.electronic_translation: Compose the album's FINAL TRACK as deliberate resolution, not as another DJ-tool. Compositional decisions serve the closing function: extended ambient outro (4-6 min sustained drone tail), beat-less coda after the album's most aggressive climax, slow tempo than rest of album (110 BPM after 130 BPM tracks), restrained density (3-4 elements vs. 8+ on peak tracks). Ending choices: long fade-to-silence (60+ sec), sustained chord on Diva pad held while reverb decays into noise floor, or single piano/cello note (Spitfire LABS) under field-recording ambience. Apply for: ambient album closers (Eno 'Music for Airports' final piece tradition), neo-classical-electronic concept-album endings (Olafur Arnalds / Nils Frahm closing pieces), techno/IDM concept-album resolutions (Aphex Twin 'Selected Ambient Works II' closers), trip-hop emotionally weighted album closers. The track's role extends BEYOND its runtime — listener should feel the ALBUM is resolved, not just the song.
album_closer_resolution_function.applicability_text: Final tracks on concept albums; tracks intended as album resolution rather than singles; compositions where song meaning extends beyond the song's runtime to inform the album's interpretation.
album_closer_resolution_function.trade_offs: Demands album-level compositional thinking, not just per-track. Won't apply to singles or non-album projects.
album_closer_resolution_function.documented_example: All Apologies as In Utero album closer — its structural function is the album's resolution, not just the song's.

litt_subtle_remix_for_chamber_arrangemen.rule: When commercial polish is needed on a CHAMBER-style arrangement (clean guitar + strings + restrained drums + vocal), apply Litt-style SUBTLE intervention rather than Wallace-style heavy compression. Light EQ + light reverb + vocal forward + minimal master compression. Polish without obliterating intimacy.
litt_subtle_remix_for_chamber_arrangemen.electronic_translation: When commercial polish is needed on an INTIMATE electronic arrangement (Rhodes/piano + sampled strings + restrained programmed drums + close-mic vocal), apply SUBTLE-INTERVENTION mix rather than aggressive mix-bus glue. Light shelf EQ (FabFilter Pro-Q3, +1dB at 12kHz for air; -1dB at 200Hz for vocal clarity), light convolution reverb (1.5s chamber, vocal forward), minimal master compression (Vari-Mu 1-2dB GR), preserve dynamic range (DR 11+, target -12 LUFS not -8). Polish without obliterating intimacy. Apply for: neo-classical-electronic mastering (Olafur Arnalds / Nils Frahm dynamic-preservation tradition), trip-hop ballad masters (Massive Attack 'Teardrop'-adjacent), ambient-with-melody pieces, electronic-pop emotionally intimate closers, downtempo evening-listening albums. The mix philosophy: every dB of compression must justify itself against the cost in intimacy. AVOID for peak-time techno (genre demands forward, glued, loud), EBM (commercial-force expected), festival masters.
litt_subtle_remix_for_chamber_arrangemen.applicability_text: Chamber-music ballad mixes; songs combining acoustic + electric elements; arrangements where the vocal IS the instrument and other instruments support it.
litt_subtle_remix_for_chamber_arrangemen.trade_offs: Requires mix engineer with subtlety + restraint capacity. Less commercial-impact than Wallace-style polish.
litt_subtle_remix_for_chamber_arrangemen.documented_example: Scott Litt's remix of All Apologies for In Utero radio single — subtle intervention preserving the chamber arrangement's intimacy.

drum_only_intro_4_bars_establishes_ident.rule: Open the song with DRUM-KIT ALONE for 4 bars. Drummer establishes tempo, groove pocket, and aggressive intent before guitars + bass + vocal enter. Intro is not transitional — it's identity-establishing. Listener locks onto the drummer's voice first.
drum_only_intro_4_bars_establishes_ident.electronic_translation: Open track with PROGRAMMED-DRUM-KIT ALONE for 4 bars (kick + snare + hats + percussion samples — no synth, no bass, no vocal). The drum programming establishes tempo, groove pocket, and aggressive intent before any harmonic content arrives. The intro is identity-establishing, not transitional — listener locks onto the rhythmic voice first. Apply on layered drum bus: tuned 808/909-derived kick, snare layer (Acetone + clap stack), hi-hats with humanized velocity (-3 to -8 dB variations), percussion fills that PROMISE the drop. Apply for: techno tracks where drum programming is the calling card (Surgeon / Robert Hood Detroit-style minimal), industrial-techno with drum-design-as-identity (Perc, Ancient Methods), IDM where rhythm-section-IS-composition (Autechre, early Squarepusher), tribal/dark-techno with intricate percussion. Pair with `syncopated_kick_creates_implied_meter_within_4_4` for rhythmic complexity within the 4-bar drum-only intro.
drum_only_intro_4_bars_establishes_ident.applicability_text: Aggressive rock tracks where drumming is signature element; songs where rhythm-section identity must be foregrounded; album tracks needing immediate sonic recognition.
drum_only_intro_4_bars_establishes_ident.trade_offs: Demands drummer with strong identity + sustained groove. Risk of feeling thin if drummer's voice underwhelming. Less suited for ballad contexts.
drum_only_intro_4_bars_establishes_ident.documented_example: Jesus Christ Pose — Matt Cameron's drum-kit-alone intro establishing JCP's relentless 4 minutes ahead of band entry.

syncopated_kick_creates_implied_meter_wi.rule: Within standard 4/4 meter, place kick on UNEXPECTED beats (offbeat 16ths, syncopated 8ths) to create an IMPLIED METER that doesn't match the literal meter. Listener perceives polymetric ambiguity while the song stays in 4/4. Compositional sophistication via rhythmic indirection.
syncopated_kick_creates_implied_meter_wi.electronic_translation: Within 4/4 grid, place kick on UNEXPECTED 16th-note positions (the '+' of beat 1, the 'e' of beat 2, the 'a' of beat 4) to create IMPLIED METER ambiguity. The track stays mathematically in 4/4 but the listener's body feels a 3-against-4 or 5-against-4 pulse. Use Ableton's MIDI editor or hardware sequencer (Elektron Analog Rytm) — disable kick from the standard four-on-the-floor positions and place it on syncopated 16ths (e.g., kick on 1, 1.75, 2.5, 3.25 across 4 beats). Apply for: IDM rhythmic complexity within club-friendly tempo (Autechre 'Amber'-era kick programming), techno-with-shuffle (Theo Parrish / Moodymann lineage), dub-techno where kick deliberately avoids the on-beat (Basic Channel / Pole tradition), glitch-funk syncopated foundations. Pair with `drum_only_intro_4_bars_establishes_identity` so listener feels the implied meter clearly before harmonic content blurs it. AVOID for peak-time festival techno (genre demands rigid four-on-the-floor for DJ-mix compatibility).
syncopated_kick_creates_implied_meter_wi.applicability_text: Aggressive rock where rhythmic complexity is desired without leaving 4/4; songs targeting 'natural complexity' (listener feels the off-balance without analyzing it); tracks with strong drummer who can sustain syncopated patterns.
syncopated_kick_creates_implied_meter_wi.trade_offs: Demands drummer with technical capacity + groove sensitivity. Risk of feeling fussy if syncopation too dense.
syncopated_kick_creates_implied_meter_wi.documented_example: JCP — Matt Cameron's kick syncopation creates implied meter within sustained 4/4 across the song.

two_guitar_stereo_separation_distinct_pl.rule: Track TWO GUITARS played by TWO DIFFERENT PLAYERS, panned hard L and hard R. Use distinct gear/voicing per player so the L vs. R guitars are sonically identifiable. Listener hears two voices, not one doubled. Compositional dimension via player identity, not just stereo width.
two_guitar_stereo_separation_distinct_pl.electronic_translation: Run TWO DISTINCT SYNTH VOICES playing complementary parts, panned hard L and hard R, using AUDIBLY DIFFERENT timbres (not the same synth duplicated). Example: Diva (saw/sub character, L100%) + Massive (FM/wavetable character, R100%) playing harmony lines or counter-melody. Each synth has its own filter/effect chain so listener hears two voices, not stereo width via doubling. Compositional dimension via TIMBRAL IDENTITY, not just stereo placement. Apply for: synthwave dual-lead arrangements (Kavinsky / Carpenter Brut tradition — two distinct synths playing harmony), electronic-pop counterpoint arrangements (Daft Punk Discovery-era L/R synth dialogues), techno with two contrasting bass-line synths in a phasing call-response, IDM where each side has a different patch character. Mono-compatibility: check phase coherence — distinct timbres typically sum well, but verify low-end summing isn't lost.
two_guitar_stereo_separation_distinct_pl.applicability_text: Two-guitar bands wanting to highlight player distinctness; songs where left/right interplay is part of the composition; tracks where stereo is a compositional axis not just a mix decision.
two_guitar_stereo_separation_distinct_pl.trade_offs: Demands two distinct guitar players + gear setups. Mono-compatibility risk. Less stereo impact than wall-of-sound multi-layered approach.
two_guitar_stereo_separation_distinct_pl.documented_example: JCP — Kim Thayil + Chris Cornell's two-guitar L/R separation, distinct gear voicings audible per side.

extended_aggressive_track_5_min_plus.rule: Sustain aggressive intensity across 5+ minutes — the LENGTH itself becomes part of the aggressive statement. Listener experiences sustained pressure as compositional choice, not endurance test. Demands material strong enough to bear extended runtime without monotony.
extended_aggressive_track_5_min_plus.electronic_translation: Sustain aggressive density across 5-7+ minutes — the LENGTH becomes part of the aggressive statement. Compose enough internal variation (filter modulations across 1-min spans, percussion-layer additions every 32 bars, micro-sound design events) to PREVENT monotony within the unrelenting frame. Apply for: industrial-techno extended workouts (Surgeon / Perc 6-min punishment tracks), peak-time techno builds (Adam Beyer / Charlotte de Witte 7-min DJ-tools), dark-electronic concept-album epics, EBM extended-mix releases. The track length IS the compositional statement: listener experiences sustained pressure as deliberate choice, not endurance test. Demands strong material — if the core groove can't sustain 5 min, shorten it rather than padding. Pair with `date_compression_balance_for_long_aggressive` to preserve dynamic detail across length, and `syncopated_kick_creates_implied_meter_within_4_4` for rhythmic interest within sustained intensity.
extended_aggressive_track_5_min_plus.applicability_text: Album tracks where aggressive identity is paramount; songs targeting Reznor/industrial sustained-intensity territory; tracks where the band must demonstrate stamina + commitment.
extended_aggressive_track_5_min_plus.trade_offs: Demands material strong enough to bear 5+ min sustained intensity. Risk of monotony if dynamic variation insufficient. Won't suit radio-singles.
extended_aggressive_track_5_min_plus.documented_example: Jesus Christ Pose at ~5:50 — sustained max-intensity across full runtime; length as compositional statement.

vocal_high_range_exploitation_as_signatu.rule: Build verse + chorus melody around the vocalist's HIGH RANGE (upper tessitura). The high-range strain becomes the song's signature element — listener associates the track with the vocalist's ceiling-pushing delivery. Different from falsetto: this is full-voice high-range, not head-voice.
vocal_high_range_exploitation_as_signatu.electronic_translation: Build melody around vocalist's UPPER FULL-VOICE TESSITURA (not falsetto/head-voice — chest-voice high range). The high-range strain becomes the track's signature element. For tracks WITHOUT a live vocalist, recreate the effect via: (a) lead-synth pitched at top of patch range for 'strained' character (Sub 37 above C5, slight pitch detuning), (b) pitched-up sample-based vocals (Granulator II / Simpler with up-tuning to convert lower-register vocal samples into upper-register signature element), (c) vocoder/Operator FM lead designed to evoke vocal-strain timbre. Apply for: dark-electronic tracks with vocalist (signature delivery as track-identity), trip-hop builds where vocalist pushes ceiling at peak moments, electronic-pop choruses pitched at top of singer's range, synthwave power-ballad-adjacent peak moments. Pair with `screamed_single_take_vocal_no_doubling` for industrial-techno aggressive tracks. AVOID for ambient/dub-techno (genres incompatible with vocal-strain aesthetic).
vocal_high_range_exploitation_as_signatu.applicability_text: Songs centered on vocal acrobatics; tracks where vocal performance IS the compositional statement; vocalists with documented high-range capacity (Cornell, Plant, Tate territory).
vocal_high_range_exploitation_as_signatu.trade_offs: Demands vocalist with high-range capacity + sustainable strain. Won't suit baritone or limited-range vocalists.
vocal_high_range_exploitation_as_signatu.documented_example: JCP — Cornell's high-range full-voice strain across the song's full runtime as signature element.

date_compression_balance_for_long_aggres.rule: When tracking + mixing extended-form aggressive material, use MODERATE compression rather than maximum — preserve dynamic detail across 5+ minutes so the aggression doesn't become flat. Terry Date's documented Badmotorfinger approach: compression for impact, but with dynamic headroom for the song's shape to read.
date_compression_balance_for_long_aggres.electronic_translation: When mixing extended-form (5+ min) aggressive electronic material, use MODERATE mix-bus compression (Vari-Mu 2dB GR, slow attack 30ms, medium release) rather than max-glue (4dB+ GR with fast attack). The MODERATE setting preserves transient detail and internal dynamic shape across the long form so the track's narrative reads. Master at -9 to -10 LUFS rather than -7 (less commercial-loudness, more dynamic detail). Apply for: techno DJ-tools (need dynamic detail for cue-mix transitions), industrial-techno album-tracks (long form demands shape preservation), dark-electronic concept-album epics (each section's dynamic differentiation must survive the master). Different from `wallace_compressed_brightness` (which glues aggressively for short impactful tracks): this preserves dynamic detail because the LENGTH demands internal shape. Tools: SSL G-Bus emulation (1-2dB GR), Vari-Mu (2dB GR), avoid digital limiters at master output. Pair with `extended_aggressive_track_5_min_plus`.
date_compression_balance_for_long_aggres.applicability_text: Long aggressive tracks needing dynamic preservation; mixes where over-compression would flatten the song's internal pacing; tracker-mixer dual-role workflows.
date_compression_balance_for_long_aggres.trade_offs: Less radio-impact than maximum compression. Demands mix engineer with dynamic-restraint capacity.
date_compression_balance_for_long_aggres.documented_example: Terry Date's mix of JCP — moderate compression preserving the song's internal dynamic shape across 5:50.

meter_shift_6_4_verse_to_4_4_chorus.rule: Compose verse in 6/4 (or other asymmetric/extended meter) and chorus in 4/4 (or vice versa). Meter SHIFT between sections marks structural change without explicit announcement. Listener feels the section change rhythmically before recognizing it intellectually.
meter_shift_6_4_verse_to_4_4_chorus.electronic_translation: Compose A-section in 6/4 (or 7/8, 5/4) and B-section in 4/4 — the meter shift marks structural change WITHOUT volume/density announcement. Set time-signature changes in the DAW (Ableton's per-segment time-sig markers, or Logic's signature track) so the grid actually changes — don't just suggest the shift in the listener's ear. Apply for: IDM compositions where meter is part of the puzzle (Autechre, Squarepusher 'Big Loada'-era), neo-classical-electronic with classical-tradition meter freedom (Olafur Arnalds 'Found Songs' rubato-adjacent), techno-experimental peak-time pieces with one mid-track meter shift surprise, dark-electronic concept-album compositional ambition. The listener feels the section change RHYTHMICALLY before recognizing it intellectually. Pair with `seven_four_asymmetric_meter_as_structural_device` for full meter-as-compositional-tool tracks. AVOID for festival-techno DJ-tools (genre demands rigid 4/4 for mixing).
meter_shift_6_4_verse_to_4_4_chorus.applicability_text: Songs wanting structural sophistication via meter rather than via dynamic shift; tracks where compositional ambition extends beyond standard 4/4; verse-chorus contrasts that should feel different in body, not just in volume.
meter_shift_6_4_verse_to_4_4_chorus.trade_offs: Demands rhythm section comfortable in multiple meters + smooth transitions. Risk of feeling fussy if shifts not natural.
meter_shift_6_4_verse_to_4_4_chorus.documented_example: Fell On Black Days — verse in 6/4 shifts to 4/4 chorus, structural change marked rhythmically.

sus_voicings_verse_with_drop_tuning_peda.rule: In verse sections, use SUSPENDED chord voicings (sus2, sus4, modal voicings without 3rd) over a DROP-TUNED bass pedal. Suspended voicings keep harmony OPEN; drop-tuned pedal anchors low end. Verse harmony floats; chorus can resolve via 3rd.
sus_voicings_verse_with_drop_tuning_peda.electronic_translation: In A-section, voice synth-pads with SUSPENDED chord voicings (sus2, sus4, no-3rd voicings — e.g., A-D-E without C# = Asus4) over a SUSTAINED LOW SUB-BASS pedal (Diva sub voice or Sub 37 sub-osc held on root tonic across the section). The suspended voicings keep harmony FLOATING/AMBIGUOUS; the sub-bass pedal anchors low end so the suspension doesn't feel rootless. B-section can resolve to triads (introduce the 3rd) for harmonic arrival. Apply for: ambient with harmonic motion (Eno-tradition modal openness), neo-classical-electronic suspended-then-resolving compositions (Nils Frahm 'Says'-adjacent), trip-hop with drone-foundation harmony (Massive Attack 'Teardrop' suspended pads), dub-techno with low-pedal-as-anchor + suspended chord washes above. Pair with `drone_foundation_as_compositional_anchor` for full suspended-over-drone aesthetic.
sus_voicings_verse_with_drop_tuning_peda.applicability_text: Verses needing modal openness; songs where harmonic ambiguity is desired in verse but resolved in chorus; drop-tuned guitar contexts.
sus_voicings_verse_with_drop_tuning_peda.trade_offs: Demands tuning + voicing literacy. Risk of feeling vague if too many sections suspended without resolution.
sus_voicings_verse_with_drop_tuning_peda.documented_example: Fell On Black Days verses — suspended voicings over drop-tuned pedal create harmonic openness; chorus resolves.

clean_to_distorted_dynamic_shift_traditi.rule: Verse: clean (or lightly overdriven) guitar tone. Chorus: full distortion. The TONE SHIFT marks dynamic change, often paired with vocal projection lift. Traditional rock dynamic-arc mechanism — quiet-loud as guitar-tone-driven structure.
clean_to_distorted_dynamic_shift_traditi.electronic_translation: A-section: clean synth tone (low-Q filter, minimal saturation, no distortion). B-section: SAME synth patch but with bypass-switched DISTORTION + filter-Q bump + drive saturation cascade engaged. The TIMBRE SHIFT marks the structural change without changing pitch material. In Ableton: chain Saturator + Drum Buss + Pedal in series on the synth track, automate their wet/dry across the section boundary. Apply on Diva or Massive lead patches. Apply for: synthwave verse-chorus structures (clean Juno-tone verse → distorted lead chorus), electronic-pop with dynamic-arc choruses (Daft Punk 'Aerodynamic'-adjacent timbral lift), trip-hop quiet-loud (Portishead clean piano verse → fuzzed-synth chorus territory), dark-electronic clean-to-aggressive transitions. Pair with `obrien_moderate_compression_for_dynamic_shift_songs` so the dynamic contrast survives mastering. AVOID for ambient (no chorus) and dub-techno (gradual rather than section-binary).
clean_to_distorted_dynamic_shift_traditi.applicability_text: Verse-chorus rock structures where dynamic shift is the song's mechanism; tracks following Pixies/Nirvana quiet-loud lineage; songs needing chorus 'lift' without volume mastering tricks.
clean_to_distorted_dynamic_shift_traditi.trade_offs: Genre-template mechanism. Risk of feeling formulaic if not paired with stronger compositional choices.
clean_to_distorted_dynamic_shift_traditi.documented_example: Fell On Black Days — clean verse to distorted chorus, Pixies/Nirvana-lineage quiet-loud mechanism.

obrien_moderate_compression_for_dynamic_.rule: When mixing dynamic-arc songs (verse quiet, chorus loud), use MODERATE compression rather than maximum — preserve the quiet-loud contrast that the composition relies on. Brendan O'Brien's documented Superunknown approach: shape impact at chorus without flattening verse intimacy.
obrien_moderate_compression_for_dynamic_.electronic_translation: When mixing dynamic-arc electronic tracks (quiet A-section, loud B-section), use MIX-BUS COMPRESSION TUNED FOR PRESERVATION rather than glue: SSL G-bus or Vari-Mu set to 1-2dB GR ON CHORUS PEAKS but releasing fully on verses (slow-attack 30ms, fast-release 100ms). The verse breathes; the chorus glues. Master at -10 to -11 LUFS rather than -7 (commercial-loudness will kill the dynamic delta the composition depends on). Apply for: synthwave verse-chorus tracks, electronic-pop with dynamic-arc choruses (Daft Punk Discovery LUFS-conservative masters), trip-hop with quiet-loud (Portishead masters preserve verse intimacy), neo-classical-electronic emotional dynamic-arc pieces. Verify on small speakers: the contrast must read on phones, not just on monitors. Pair with `clean_to_distorted_dynamic_shift_traditional` for dynamic-arc compositions.
obrien_moderate_compression_for_dynamic_.applicability_text: Mix decisions on dynamic-arc material; songs where over-compression would destroy the verse-chorus contrast; producer-mixer-split workflows.
obrien_moderate_compression_for_dynamic_.trade_offs: Less radio-impact than maximum compression. Demands mix engineer with restraint capacity.
obrien_moderate_compression_for_dynamic_.documented_example: Brendan O'Brien's mix of FOBD — moderate compression preserving the verse-chorus dynamic shift.

vocal_subselective_range_use.rule: Vocalist with documented wide range chooses to deploy ONLY a NARROW SUB-RANGE for the song. Restraint as compositional choice — the wide-range capacity is RESERVED rather than displayed. Listener experiences vocal restraint as artistic discipline, not lack of capacity.
vocal_subselective_range_use.electronic_translation: When vocalist has documented wide range, restrict the song's melodic line to a NARROW REGISTER (5-7 semitones max, no leaps to upper tessitura) — restraint as compositional choice. The vocal-acrobatic capacity is RESERVED for other tracks; this track's emotional weight comes from CONTAINMENT. For instrumental tracks, apply the same principle to lead synth: pick a 5-7 semitone melodic range and stay there for the entire piece — the discipline IS the statement. Apply for: trip-hop reflective tracks (Portishead 'Roads'-tradition narrow-register vocal weight), neo-classical-electronic intimate vocal moments (Olafur Arnalds collaborations with Arnor Dan, restrained delivery), ambient-with-melody pieces (the melodic line confines itself to give the harmony space), downtempo evening listening. Different from `vocal_high_range_exploitation_as_signature` (which displays range) — this RESERVES range. Listener feels the unspoken capacity beneath the restraint.
vocal_subselective_range_use.applicability_text: Songs by vocalists known for wide range (Cornell, Mercury, Plant) that benefit from sub-selective restraint; tracks where vocal acrobatics would compete with arrangement; intimate sections within otherwise dynamic albums.
vocal_subselective_range_use.trade_offs: Demands documented wide-range vocalist who restrains. Risk of feeling underwhelming if listener expects acrobatics.
vocal_subselective_range_use.documented_example: FOBD — Cornell narrows his documented wide range to a sub-selective register for the song's reflective character.

five_four_groove_natural_via_groove_cons.rule: Compose in 5/4 meter but construct the groove so it FEELS NATURAL rather than mathematically odd. Group beats as 3+2 or 2+3 and build melodic + bass phrases that spell the groupings naturally. Listener feels the lilt without analyzing the meter.
five_four_groove_natural_via_groove_cons.electronic_translation: Compose in 5/4 (set DAW time-sig to 5/4) but program drums + bass-line + melodic motifs so the groove feels NATURAL via implicit grouping (3+2 or 2+3). Kick on 1 + 4 (3+2 grouping) or 1 + 3 (2+3 grouping). Bass-line phrase resolves on the 5/4 cycle, not the 4/4 cycle the listener might expect. Build melodic phrases that spell the grouping naturally so listener feels the LILT without analyzing the meter. Apply for: IDM 5/4 groove-tracks (early Squarepusher, Aphex Twin 'Donkey Rhubarb' 5/4 sections), techno-experimental with one 5/4 mid-track section, electronic-pop oddmeter signature songs (Radiohead '15 Step' 5/4-adjacent influence on electronic-pop), neo-classical-electronic with classical odd-meter tradition. The DISCIPLINE: the listener should be able to body-rock to the 5/4 without counting; if they're counting, the groove construction failed.
five_four_groove_natural_via_groove_cons.applicability_text: Songs wanting odd-meter character without virtuosic complexity; tracks where rhythmic playfulness is the goal; bands with rhythm sections fluent in odd meters.
five_four_groove_natural_via_groove_cons.trade_offs: Demands rhythm section comfortable in 5/4 + groove-construction craft. Risk of feeling academic if grouping not naturalized.
five_four_groove_natural_via_groove_cons.documented_example: My Wave 5/4 throughout — Cameron's documented natural 5/4 groove (3+2 grouping) feels playful rather than odd.

drum_meter_pattern_groupings_natural_vs_.rule: When drumming odd meter (5/4, 7/4), choose the GROUPING that feels NATURAL to the song rather than the academically 'correct' one. 5/4 can be 3+2, 2+3, or even 1+1+1+1+1. Drummer's choice of grouping shapes the song's rhythmic identity.
drum_meter_pattern_groupings_natural_vs_.electronic_translation: When programming drums in odd meter (5/4, 7/4, 7/8), choose the IMPLIED GROUPING that fits the song's emotional intent rather than the academically default one. 5/4 can be 3+2 (rolling, dance-friendly), 2+3 (off-balance, tension-charged), or 1+1+1+1+1 (mechanical, IDM-aesthetic). 7/4 can be 4+3 (groove-leaning), 3+4 (Yes-prog-tradition), 2+2+3 (additive, shuffle-feel), or 3+2+2 (riff-spelling). Each grouping shapes the track's rhythmic identity differently. In Ableton: program kick + snare placements that AUDIBLY spell the grouping (kick on the first beat of each group); use velocity accents to reinforce the implied subdivision. Apply for: IDM rhythm-design tracks where grouping IS the puzzle, techno-experimental peak-time pieces with one signature odd-meter section, dub-techno additive-meter compositions, electronic-pop oddmeter signature songs. Pair with `five_four_groove_natural_via_groove_construction`.
drum_meter_pattern_groupings_natural_vs_.applicability_text: Odd-meter compositions where drummer has interpretive freedom; tracks where groove feel matters more than theoretical clarity; bands with drummers who internalize odd meters bodily.
drum_meter_pattern_groupings_natural_vs_.trade_offs: Demands drummer with internalized odd-meter capacity. Risk of feeling stiff if grouping forced.
drum_meter_pattern_groupings_natural_vs_.documented_example: My Wave — Cameron groups 5/4 as 3+2 naturally, drum pattern spells the grouping rather than counting it.

same_team_different_mix_per_song.rule: Within an album session, the SAME engineer-producer team can apply DIFFERENT MIX PHILOSOPHIES per song based on each song's compositional intent. No single mix template imposed across the album. Producer's craft is interpretive, not template-driven.
same_team_different_mix_per_song.electronic_translation: On an album/EP project where YOU produce all tracks (or you + co-producer), apply DIFFERENT MIX PHILOSOPHIES per track based on each track's compositional intent — NOT a single album-wide mix template. Track 1 (peak-time): commercial-force chain (Vari-Mu 4dB GR, brightness shelf, -7 LUFS master). Track 2 (ambient interlude): raw-capture chain (no mix-bus comp, -14 LUFS, preserved spatial decay). Track 3 (intimate vocal piece): subtle-intervention chain (light EQ, dynamic preservation, -11 LUFS). Each track's mix decision serves ITS intent, not album consistency. Apply for: concept-album EPs (techno + ambient interludes coexisting), electronic-pop albums with ballads + bangers, neo-classical-electronic albums with dynamic-arc + sustained pieces, downtempo collections with track-specific shaping. The album's COHERENCE comes from compositional voice + sonic palette, NOT from uniform mastering.
same_team_different_mix_per_song.applicability_text: Albums with diverse compositional intents per track; producer-band partnerships where producer has stylistic range; sessions where each song deserves its own mix decision.
same_team_different_mix_per_song.trade_offs: Demands producer with stylistic range + flexibility. Risk of album feeling inconsistent if mix variation too wide.
same_team_different_mix_per_song.documented_example: Beinhorn + O'Brien on Superunknown — same team, different mix philosophy per song depending on compositional intent.

bassist_dual_role_within_album.rule: Bassist plays DIFFERENT ROLES on different album tracks — supporting bass on some songs, melodically-leading bass on others, even compositional driver on co-written tracks. Bassist's range expands the album's compositional palette. Not just rhythm-section anchor.
bassist_dual_role_within_album.electronic_translation: The BASS-LINE plays DIFFERENT ROLES across the album/EP — supporting sub-bass anchor on some tracks, melodically-leading lead bass on others, compositional driver on at least one. Track 1: 808 sub-bass simply doubling the kick (rhythmic anchor). Track 2: Sub 37 melodic bass-line that drives the harmonic motion (compositional foreground). Track 3: TB-303-style acid bass that IS the track's hook (lead role). Track 4: pad-bass that blends with the mid-range pads (textural support). The bass's RANGE OF FUNCTION expands the album's compositional palette beyond rhythm-section anchor. Apply for: techno EPs with bass as primary identity-shaper, electronic-pop albums where bass-character defines tracks, dark-electronic concept albums with bass-foregrounded showcases, IDM compositional collections. Each role demands different processing: anchor-bass needs sidechain to kick; lead-bass needs character/saturation; compositional-driver bass needs harmonic clarity.
bassist_dual_role_within_album.applicability_text: Albums where bassist has compositional contributions; tracks where bass is foreground (vs. supporting) for specific songs; bands willing to feature bass beyond rhythm-section role.
bassist_dual_role_within_album.trade_offs: Demands bassist with multi-role capacity. Risk of incoherent bass identity if roles too varied.
bassist_dual_role_within_album.documented_example: Ben Shepherd on Superunknown — bass plays supporting role on My Wave but compositional driver on Head Down.

tracking_priority_per_song_compositional.rule: At tracking stage, the PRIORITY ELEMENT changes per song based on compositional intent. One song prioritizes guitar wall; another prioritizes drum feel; another prioritizes vocal restraint. Engineer-producer adapts tracking priorities per song rather than imposing template.
tracking_priority_per_song_compositional.electronic_translation: At PRODUCTION stage (track-1 in Ableton), the PRIORITY ELEMENT changes per track based on compositional intent. Track 1 (peak-time techno): start production from kick + sub design (rhythm-section anchor); spend 60% of session on drum-bus voicing. Track 2 (ambient piece): start from pad/drone design; spend 60% of session on harmonic textures + reverb sculpting. Track 3 (electronic-pop song): start from vocal arrangement + chord-progression; spend 60% on song-form + vocal processing. Each track's CRAFT TIME goes to its identity-defining element. Apply for: producer-as-self EPs/albums where each track demands different production focus, multi-track concept albums where homogeneous workflow flattens the album's range. The DISCIPLINE: identify each track's identity element BEFORE starting, then commit production time accordingly. AVOID for tracks of similar genre — applying this principle within a single homogeneous techno EP is overkill.
tracking_priority_per_song_compositional.applicability_text: Albums with diverse compositional intents; producer-band relationships where producer can interpret per-song needs; sessions willing to spend extra time on song-specific tracking decisions.
tracking_priority_per_song_compositional.trade_offs: Adds session time + complexity. Demands producer with interpretive flexibility.
tracking_priority_per_song_compositional.documented_example: Beinhorn's Superunknown tracking — different priority per song (My Wave drum-feel vs. JCP guitar-wall vs. Black Hole Sun vocal).

extended_atmospheric_form_6_minutes_plus.rule: Build a 6+ minute rock track around an EXTENDED MIDDLE SECTION (3+ minutes of instrumental exploration). Verses + choruses become FRAMING for the central instrumental statement. Vocal absent for substantial duration trusts the band to carry listener attention via texture + harmonic motion.
extended_atmospheric_form_6_minutes_plus.electronic_translation: Compose a 6-8+ min track around an EXTENDED MIDDLE SECTION (3+ minutes of beat-less or instrumental exploration). Vocal/lead-synth sections at the start and end become FRAMING for the central instrumental statement — the bridge IS the song. The extended middle uses textural evolution (granular sample beds via Granulator II, evolving filter modulations on Diva pads, subtle automation of reverb size from 4s to 12s, layered field-recordings) to carry attention without dynamic-arc rescue. Apply for: ambient-with-form pieces (Brian Eno 'On Land'-tradition extended atmospheric forms), neo-classical-electronic 7-min compositions (Olafur Arnalds extended pieces), techno-experimental concept tracks (deep-techno extended workouts), trip-hop epics (Massive Attack 'Unfinished Sympathy'-adjacent extended forms), IDM concept-album centerpieces. The compositional discipline: the middle section must STAND on its own as compelling music — without the framing sections, the bridge should still hold a listener.
extended_atmospheric_form_6_minutes_plus.applicability_text: Long-form rock/post-rock/psychedelic compositions where the listener should be carried through extended instrumental exploration. Use when the SONG'S IDENTITY is the bridge, not the verses/choruses.
extended_atmospheric_form_6_minutes_plus.trade_offs: Requires excellent musicians + atmospheric instrumental writing. Won't work in radio-format contexts.
extended_atmospheric_form_6_minutes_plus.documented_example: Overfloater (Soundgarden, Down on the Upside 1996, 6:42) — extended instrumental bridge is the song's identity.

instrumental_bridge_as_song_centerpiece_.rule: Make the BRIDGE the song's structural center (longest section, peak moment) rather than treating it as a transition. Bridge length should EXCEED chorus or verse length combined. Bridge harmonic motion should DIVERGE from verse/chorus pattern (modal, suspended, exploratory).
instrumental_bridge_as_song_centerpiece_.electronic_translation: Make the BREAKDOWN/BRIDGE section the track's STRUCTURAL CENTER — its longest single section, its peak emotional moment — rather than treating it as a transition between verse-chorus iterations. Bridge length should EXCEED any verse or chorus combined (3-min bridge in a 6-min track). Bridge harmonic motion should DIVERGE from verse/chorus chord progression — go modal (move from major to Aeolian), introduce suspended pads (sus2/sus4 voicings), or shift to exploratory chord movement (parallel 4ths, planing Lydian). Apply for: trip-hop with extended bridges as song-identity (Portishead breakdowns), electronic-pop with structural-bridge ambition (Daft Punk 'Aerodynamic' extended middle), IDM compositions where the middle section IS the puzzle, neo-classical-electronic emotional centerpiece pieces, deep-techno breakdowns that become the track's emotional weight. The bridge must STAND on its own — without the framing verse-chorus, the bridge should still hold a listener.
instrumental_bridge_as_song_centerpiece_.applicability_text: Compositions where the bridge is the song's identity; tracks where instrumental exploration deserves more compositional space than vocal sections.
instrumental_bridge_as_song_centerpiece_.trade_offs: Requires bridge to STAND on its own as compelling music — can't just be 'the part between sections'.
instrumental_bridge_as_song_centerpiece_.documented_example: Overfloater extended bridge — multiple minutes long, harmonically distinct from verse/chorus.

jam_band_influence_in_rock_format.rule: Borrow extended-form rock traditions (Pink Floyd, Led Zeppelin's longer tracks, Phish-adjacent jam sensibility) into a hard-rock or alt-rock context. Allow space for instrumental exploration without rushing back to vocal sections. The BAND's collective improvisation/arrangement carries the listener.
jam_band_influence_in_rock_format.electronic_translation: Borrow EXTENDED-FORM EXPLORATION (Pink Floyd 'Echoes', krautrock-tradition motorik patience, Eno-Cluster ambient drift) into a structured electronic context. Allow extended instrumental exploration without rushing back to verse/drop sections. Evolve the central section through automation curves spanning minutes (filter cutoff sweeping over 90 sec, reverb size morphing 4s → 12s over 60 sec, granular grain-density modulating across 2 min) so the listener is CARRIED rather than punctuated. Apply for: ambient pieces (Tangerine Dream / Eno extended-form lineage), neo-classical-electronic with patient development (Olafur Arnalds 'Sudden Throw' extended forms), techno-experimental with krautrock-influenced patience (Donato Dozzy lineage), dub-techno extended cycles (Basic Channel 'Phylyps Trak'-tradition meditative repetition), IDM jam-influenced compositions. The discipline: trust the listener to sustain attention via texture rather than rescuing them with the next section.
jam_band_influence_in_rock_format.applicability_text: Hard-rock/alt-rock compositions seeking extended-form ambition via jam-band tradition; bands with collective improvisation capacity; tracks willing to embrace cross-genre influence.
jam_band_influence_in_rock_format.trade_offs: Genre-crossing; risk alienating listeners expecting tight rock economy.
jam_band_influence_in_rock_format.documented_example: Overfloater bridge — jam-band-influenced extended texture in a hard-rock catalog.

vocal_absent_extended_section_trust_the_.rule: Have NO VOCAL for an extended section (2+ minutes) in a vocal-driven song. The instrumental texture must be compelling enough to hold attention without lyrical anchor. Used as DRAMATIC choice — the absence of voice becomes a structural statement.
vocal_absent_extended_section_trust_the_.electronic_translation: Have NO VOCAL/no lead-melody for an extended section (2+ minutes) in an otherwise vocal-driven track. The instrumental TEXTURE must be compelling enough to hold attention without melodic-anchor. Used as DRAMATIC structural device — the absence of the lead becomes a statement. Build the texture from: evolving pad layers (Diva sustained chord wash with slow LFO modulation), percussive elements that carry rhythmic interest (subtle hi-hat patterns + filter-modulated percussion samples), bass-line that becomes melodic foreground in the lead's absence, evolving granular textures (Granulator II grain-density automation). Apply for: trip-hop extended bridges (Massive Attack 'Unfinished Sympathy' extended instrumental sections), electronic-pop ambitious form (Daft Punk Discovery extended bridges), neo-classical-electronic vocal-then-instrumental compositions, IDM songs where the lead is RESERVED to specific moments and the texture carries the rest. The DISCIPLINE: the instrumental section must STAND on its own — without the surrounding vocal sections, the bridge should still feel like compelling music.
vocal_absent_extended_section_trust_the_.applicability_text: Vocal-driven songs willing to sustain instrumental sections; tracks where listener should be carried by band texture; compositions wanting structural drama via vocal absence.
vocal_absent_extended_section_trust_the_.trade_offs: Listeners conditioned to expect vocal hooks may disengage. Requires confident instrumental writing.
vocal_absent_extended_section_trust_the_.documented_example: Overfloater multi-minute vocal-absent bridge — listener trusts band to carry them through.

kasper_band_driven_unified_engineering_m.rule: Use UNIFIED engineering + mixing roles (one person tracks AND mixes) to preserve recording-stage decisions through to final master. Avoid the producer-driven external-mixer split when the band's tracking aesthetic IS the desired final aesthetic. Result: more authentic, less sonically-shaped final product.
kasper_band_driven_unified_engineering_m.electronic_translation: ONE PERSON (you, or you + close collaborator) handles production AND mix AND mastering — preserving production-stage sonic decisions through to final master. Avoid the producer-then-external-mixing-engineer split when YOUR production aesthetic IS the desired final sound. The advantage: tonal/spatial decisions made during synthesis-design carry through to master without external interpretation. Apply for: solo electronic-producer projects (the entire Reznor/Daft Punk auteur tradition), bedroom-producer EPs where the rough character IS the aesthetic, ambient/experimental projects where outsider-mix would smooth-out compelling rough edges, dub-techno where production-phase decisions about reverb/saturation are inseparable from the final aesthetic. AVOID for commercial pop tracks targeting radio (where external mix-engineer translation matters), or when YOU lack mixing/mastering expertise — in those cases the external pair of ears is worth the aesthetic compromise. Pair with `daft_punk_duo_auteur_home_studio` philosophy.
kasper_band_driven_unified_engineering_m.applicability_text: Late-career bands who know their sound; capture-focused projects; projects where authenticity matters more than commercial polish.
kasper_band_driven_unified_engineering_m.trade_offs: Less external sonic shaping; less radio-optimized; more authenticity-leaning. Requires excellent tracking decisions upfront.
kasper_band_driven_unified_engineering_m.documented_example: Adam Kasper engineered + co-produced + mixed Down on the Upside (1996) — band-driven, engineer-mixer unified role.

atmospheric_continuity_over_dynamic_cont.rule: Build a song where SUSTAINED ATMOSPHERIC CONTINUITY is the primary aesthetic, NOT quiet/loud dynamic contrast. Sections shift via harmonic + textural variation rather than via volume + intensity changes. Reverb + sustain + spacious mix support this.
atmospheric_continuity_over_dynamic_cont.electronic_translation: Build a track where SUSTAINED ATMOSPHERIC CONTINUITY is the primary aesthetic — NO quiet/loud dynamic contrast. Sections shift via HARMONIC + TEXTURAL VARIATION (chord-color shift from Aeolian to Dorian, texture-density change from 4 layers to 7 layers, timbre evolution via filter modulation) rather than via volume/intensity changes. Sustain everything via long reverb tails (4-12s convolution reverb on pads), preserved decay (no aggressive gate), spacious mix that breathes. Apply for: ambient pieces (Eno, Stars of the Lid, William Basinski lineage), dub-techno extended workouts (Basic Channel/Pole — section change via texture not volume), dark-ambient compositions (Lustmord, Atrium Carceri), neo-classical-electronic sustained pieces (Olafur Arnalds 'Found Songs' atmospheric continuity), drone-techno meditative tracks. Different from `extended_dynamic_arc` — this REJECTS dynamic-arc as the structural mechanism and substitutes textural continuity. Pair with `harmonic_minimalism_one_cycle_repeated` for sustained-atmosphere compositions.
atmospheric_continuity_over_dynamic_cont.applicability_text: Atmospheric tracks where dynamic shifts would feel cheap; songs prioritizing texture/harmony over architecture; bands with mix engineer who can sustain atmospheric depth.
atmospheric_continuity_over_dynamic_cont.trade_offs: Less obvious 'rock dynamics' for listeners conditioned to verse/chorus contrast. Requires harmonic + textural interest to sustain.
atmospheric_continuity_over_dynamic_cont.documented_example: Overfloater — atmospheric continuity across 6:42, no quiet/loud dynamic contrast as structural device.

seven_four_asymmetric_meter_as_structura.rule: Use 7/4 (or other long asymmetric meters: 11/8, 13/8) as STRUCTURAL DEVICE rather than as decorative complexity. The long asymmetric cycle should feel HYPNOTIC + STATELY — listeners inhabit the cycle. 7-beat groupings can subdivide as 4+3, 3+4, 3+2+2, or 2+3+2 depending on rhythmic emphasis.
seven_four_asymmetric_meter_as_structura.electronic_translation: Use 7/4 (or 11/8, 13/8) as STRUCTURAL DEVICE — slow tempo (80-95 BPM), long cycle, HYPNOTIC + STATELY feel, listener inhabits the cycle rather than counts it. Set DAW time-sig to 7/4. Choose grouping that fits intent: 4+3 (rolling, dance-friendly), 3+4 (off-balance, prog-tradition), 3+2+2 (riff-spelling), 2+3+2 (lilting). Program drum patterns + bass-line + chord-pad changes that AUDIBLY spell the chosen grouping. Apply for: drone-techno meditative pieces (Donato Dozzy / Voices From The Lake-tradition long cycles in odd meter), ambient with rhythmic foundation (Aphex Twin 'Ambient Works II'-adjacent slow pulses in 7/4), IDM compositional ambition (Autechre 'Garbagemx36'-tradition long-cycle pieces), neo-classical-electronic with classical odd-meter heritage. Different from `five_four_groove_natural` — this is meter-as-MEDITATION rather than meter-as-LILT. Pair with `drone_foundation_as_compositional_anchor`.
seven_four_asymmetric_meter_as_structura.applicability_text: Atmospheric rock/post-rock/drone-doom-adjacent compositions where asymmetric meter + drone harmony serve meditative or hypnotic intent.
seven_four_asymmetric_meter_as_structura.trade_offs: Requires excellent drummer + bassist to make asymmetric meter FEEL natural rather than counted-stiffly. Listeners conditioned to 4/4 may need time to lock in.
seven_four_asymmetric_meter_as_structura.documented_example: Head Down (Soundgarden, Superunknown 1994, 7/4 throughout) — Cameron's 7/4 mastery makes it feel natural rather than arithmetic.

drone_foundation_as_compositional_anchor.rule: Build a song where SUSTAINED LOW-FREQUENCY DRONE is the harmonic gravity well. All other harmonic motion happens ABOVE the drone (suspended voicings, modal inflections) rather than via traditional chord progression. Drone should be present 80%+ of song duration.
drone_foundation_as_compositional_anchor.electronic_translation: Build a track around a SUSTAINED LOW-FREQUENCY DRONE as harmonic gravity-well. Layer the drone from: (a) Diva or Sub 37 sub-osc held on root (Eb1 or D1), (b) Spitfire LABS Soft Piano sustained low note, (c) field-recording rumble (industrial-machinery low end), (d) bass synth pad with very slow LFO modulation. Drone present 80%+ of track duration. All other harmonic motion happens ABOVE the drone — suspended voicings, modal inflections, slowly shifting upper-pad colors — rather than via traditional chord progression. The drone's psychoacoustic effect: listener's body locks to the low frequency, perceiving harmonic motion above as RELATIVE rather than absolute. Apply for: dark-ambient compositions (Lustmord, Thomas Köner — drone IS the piece), drone-techno (Mika Vainio, Pan Sonic tradition), neo-classical-electronic with sustained tonic foundation (Stars of the Lid extended drones), dub-techno with low-bass-anchor (Basic Channel signature low pedal), industrial-techno meditative tracks. Pair with `modal_voicings_above_drone_replace_chord_progression`.
drone_foundation_as_compositional_anchor.applicability_text: Atmospheric rock or drone-doom-adjacent compositions; tracks where harmonic stasis + textural exploration serve compositional intent.
drone_foundation_as_compositional_anchor.trade_offs: Requires excellent bass tone + sustain capability. May feel monotonous to listeners expecting harmonic motion.
drone_foundation_as_compositional_anchor.documented_example: Head Down — Shepherd's sustained low B drone foundation across 6:08.

modal_voicings_above_drone_replace_chord.rule: Instead of standard chord progression (I-IV-V-I or i-VI-VII-i), use SUSTAINED MODAL VOICINGS above a drone foundation. Suspensions (sus2, sus4), modal inflections (Aeolian, Phrygian), and quartal/quintal voicings preserve harmonic openness while creating subtle motion.
modal_voicings_above_drone_replace_chord.electronic_translation: Above a sustained drone (low D pedal), use SUSTAINED MODAL PAD VOICINGS rather than I-IV-V-I or i-VI-VII-i progressions. Layer: suspended voicings (Dsus2 = D-E-A, Dsus4 = D-G-A), modal inflections (D Aeolian = D-F-A, D Phrygian = D-Eb-A creating dark-modal color), quartal voicings (D-G-C, stacked 4ths — contemporary jazz tradition), quintal voicings (D-A-E, open + ambiguous). Voice on Diva or Massive pad patches with slow attack (200-500 ms) and long release (3-5 sec) so voicings BLEND across changes rather than punctuating. Apply for: ambient with harmonic interest beyond static drone (Stars of the Lid 'And Their Refinement of the Decline' modal layering), neo-classical-electronic with classical-modal vocabulary (Olafur Arnalds modal piano + synth pad voicings), dub-techno chord-pad work above bass-pedal (Basic Channel modal voicing tradition), drone-techno with subtle harmonic motion. Different from chord progressions — this is harmonic STASIS WITH SUBTLE COLOR-SHIFT.
modal_voicings_above_drone_replace_chord.applicability_text: Compositions with drone foundation; tracks where harmonic openness is desired over progression-driven motion; modal-vocabulary contexts.
modal_voicings_above_drone_replace_chord.trade_offs: Less obvious 'songwriting' for listeners conditioned to hook-driven progressions. Requires harmonic ear to write convincingly.
modal_voicings_above_drone_replace_chord.documented_example: Head Down — modal voicings above drone foundation, harmonic motion via voicing shifts not chord changes.

bassist_driven_compositional_identity.rule: Allow the BASSIST to drive primary compositional identity (riff, structure, harmonic foundation) rather than always centering guitar riffs. Documented as Shepherd's role on Head Down — the bass drone IS the compositional engine.
bassist_driven_compositional_identity.electronic_translation: Let the BASS-LINE drive primary compositional identity — the bass-line IS the track's structural engine, not the lead synth or vocal. Compose the bass-line FIRST as a complete piece (16-bar structure with internal tension/release shape), then arrange other elements AROUND it: drums fill in to support the bass's groove, lead synths echo or harmonize the bass-line's contour, pads provide harmonic context the bass implies. The bass-line's CHARACTER (TB-303 acid resonance, Sub 37 fat sub, Massive FM bass) defines the track's identity. Apply for: acid-techno where 303-line IS the track (Hardfloor / Plastikman tradition), industrial-techno bass-driven punishment tracks (Surgeon / Perc bass-as-foreground), dub-techno with bass-pedal as compositional anchor (Basic Channel / Rhythm & Sound), EBM signature bass-line tracks (Front 242 'Headhunter' bass-as-identity), funk-electro / electronic-pop where bass-line IS the song (Daft Punk 'Da Funk' tradition).
bassist_driven_compositional_identity.applicability_text: Bands willing to feature bassist as compositional driver; songs where bass-line is the song's primary identity; tracks where bassist co-writes or solo-writes.
bassist_driven_compositional_identity.trade_offs: Requires bassist with compositional vision; band must trust bassist's structural ideas.
bassist_driven_compositional_identity.documented_example: Head Down — Ben Shepherd co-wrote, bass drone foundation is compositional anchor.

long_asymmetric_cycle_for_meditation_not.rule: When using asymmetric meter (7/4, 5/4, 11/8), let the cycle feel LONG + MEDITATIVE rather than tight + complex. Slow tempos (80-95 BPM) + sustained instrumentation help listeners INHABIT the cycle rather than count it. Asymmetric meter as MEDITATIVE STRUCTURAL DEVICE not as virtuosic flex.
long_asymmetric_cycle_for_meditation_not.electronic_translation: When using asymmetric meter (7/4, 5/4, 11/8), keep tempo SLOW (75-95 BPM) and instrumentation SUSTAINED so the cycle feels LONG + MEDITATIVE rather than tight + complex. Listener INHABITS the cycle rather than counts it. Pad-heavy texture with long release times, drum patterns that ANCHOR rather than display, bass-line that breathes across the full cycle. Resist the temptation to show off the odd-meter virtuosity — the discipline IS the statement. Apply for: drone-techno meditative pieces (Donato Dozzy, Voices From The Lake — slow asymmetric cycles), ambient with rhythmic foundation (slow 7/4 pulse under pads), neo-classical-electronic with classical odd-meter heritage (Olafur Arnalds 'Sudden Throw' adjacent), dub-techno additive-meter pieces (Basic Channel slow odd-cycles), trip-hop slow-meditative tracks. Different from `five_four_groove_natural` (lilting body-rock energy) — this is meter-as-MEDITATION at slower tempo. Pair with `seven_four_asymmetric_meter_as_structural_device` and `atmospheric_continuity_over_dynamic_contrast`.
long_asymmetric_cycle_for_meditation_not.applicability_text: Atmospheric tracks using odd meter; songs where the meter should feel meditative; bands willing to slow asymmetric meter into hypnotic cycle territory.
long_asymmetric_cycle_for_meditation_not.trade_offs: Requires aesthetic restraint — temptation to show off odd-meter playing must be resisted.
long_asymmetric_cycle_for_meditation_not.documented_example: Head Down 7/4 at ~88 BPM — meditative cycle, not virtuosic complexity.

low_frequency_clarity_priority_in_drone_.rule: When mixing drone-foundation arrangements, give the drone bass + low-tuned instruments DEDICATED low-frequency space. Coordinate kick (~60Hz) + bass drone (~80-120Hz) via complementary frequency placement. Avoid over-compressing the drone — preserve its natural envelope. Light dedicated reverb on drone-frequency layers can enhance spatial depth.
low_frequency_clarity_priority_in_drone_.electronic_translation: When mixing drone-based electronic tracks, dedicate complementary FREQUENCY SLOTS to the drone components. Kick at 50-65 Hz fundamental + bass drone at 80-130 Hz fundamental — high-pass the kick at 50Hz, high-pass the drone at 75Hz, sidechain the drone to duck under kick transients (-3dB) so they don't fight. Avoid heavy multi-band compression on the drone bus — preserve its natural envelope (drone IS sustained, compression destroys the sustain character). Apply mono summing only below 100Hz (using Pro-Q3 mid-side mode); allow drone harmonics 100-300Hz to spread stereo. Light convolution reverb (3-5s, dark space) on a SEND from the drone bus, NOT inserted, so the dry drone preserves its low-end clarity while the reverb provides spatial halo. Apply for: dub-techno mixes (Basic Channel low-end clarity tradition), dark-ambient mixes where drone is the foundation, drone-techno mastering, ambient with sustained low-frequency content.
low_frequency_clarity_priority_in_drone_.applicability_text: Drone-based compositions in mix stage; tracks where low-end clarity is critical to the song's compositional foundation.
low_frequency_clarity_priority_in_drone_.trade_offs: Requires careful frequency carving + restraint with compression.
low_frequency_clarity_priority_in_drone_.documented_example: Head Down O'Brien mix — bass drone clearly audible without becoming muddy.

wall_of_layered_guitars_frequency_spread.rule: Layer multiple guitar tracks (5-15+) panned across stereo field + EQ-staggered to occupy DIFFERENT FREQUENCY SLOTS (low-mid, mid-range, upper-mid, high). Result: wall reads as ONE BIG GUITAR rather than as cluttered multiple guitars. Each layer can have different distortion character (different amps/pedals) for textural variation.
wall_of_layered_guitars_frequency_spread.electronic_translation: Build a synth-stack of 5-15 SUSTAINED PADS / processed-sample layers, each with different source (analog VA mono, granular pad from Output Substance, FM bell from Operator, bowed-string from Spitfire LABS, vocoded sustain, noise-bed from Reaktor) and EQ-staggered to occupy distinct frequency slots: layer 1 sub (40-80Hz), layer 2 low-mid (150-300Hz), layer 3 mid (500-1k), layer 4 upper-mid (2-4k), layer 5+ high (6-12k). Pan hard L / soft L / center / soft R / hard R / wide-stereo. Each layer can have different saturation character (Decapitator on one, FabFilter Saturn on another, hardware tape on a third for analog warmth). Wall reads as ONE BIG TEXTURE — same compositional principle as Quiet/Siamese Dream, but in synth-stack instead of guitar-stack. Apply for: peak chorus density in techno/trance peaks, ambient texture climax, dark-electronic drop, synthwave wall-of-sound chorus. Avoid in minimum-element-count or filter-house contexts (incompatible aesthetics).
wall_of_layered_guitars_frequency_spread.applicability_text: Heavy alt-rock or modern-rock compositions where dense guitar layering serves a tight commercial economy. Use when the SONG'S IDENTITY is the riff + the wall, not dynamic arc.
wall_of_layered_guitars_frequency_spread.trade_offs: Time-intensive to track + mix correctly. Risk of mud if EQ-staggering not careful. Requires patient producer/mixer (Vig + Wallace).
wall_of_layered_guitars_frequency_spread.documented_example: Quiet (Smashing Pumpkins, Siamese Dream 1993) + entire Siamese Dream album — Corgan's documented multi-layered guitar philosophy.

riff_as_both_hook_and_structural_backbon.rule: Write a guitar riff that is SIMULTANEOUSLY the song's hook AND its structural backbone (continues through verses + reappears in choruses). Guitar riff carries melodic + harmonic + rhythmic identity. Vocal hook becomes secondary to riff hook.
riff_as_both_hook_and_structural_backbon.electronic_translation: Write a SYNTH-RIFF (lead-bass line, acid-bass pattern, or melodic-synth motif) that is SIMULTANEOUSLY the track's hook AND its structural backbone — riff continues through verses, returns at peak. The riff carries melodic + harmonic + rhythmic identity. Voice/lead-melody becomes secondary to riff-identity. Build on TB-303 acid-line (Phoscyon, AudioRealism), Sub 37 character-bass, Massive aggressive lead, or Operator FM bell-pattern. Apply for: acid-techno where 303-line IS the song (Hardfloor 'Acperience'-tradition, Plastikman lineage), industrial-techno riff-driven tracks (Surgeon, Perc), EBM signature-bass tracks (Front 242 'Headhunter'), synthwave hook-riff compositions (Kavinsky 'Nightcall' — synth-riff IS the song), electro-funk (Daft Punk 'Da Funk' Moog riff IS the track). Listener associates the track with its riff first, lyrics/lead-vocal second. Pair with `descending_riff_as_song_identity` and `wall_of_layered_guitars_frequency_spread_layering` (synth-stack version).
riff_as_both_hook_and_structural_backbon.applicability_text: Heavy rock songs where the riff IS the song; tracks where guitar identity supersedes vocal identity; commercial-rock contexts wanting riff-driven memorability.
riff_as_both_hook_and_structural_backbon.trade_offs: Requires strong riff-writing capacity. Listeners conditioned to vocal-hook pop may need adjustment.
riff_as_both_hook_and_structural_backbon.documented_example: Quiet opening riff — both hook and structure.

sustained_intensity_no_dynamic_arc_econo.rule: Build a heavy rock song that stays HEAVY throughout — no quiet/loud dynamic contrast, no clean intro, no atmospheric bridge. Sustained intensity for full runtime (3-4 minutes for tight commercial economy). Choruses can EXPAND density but should not REDUCE intensity.
sustained_intensity_no_dynamic_arc_econo.electronic_translation: Build a track that stays at FULL DENSITY throughout (3-4 min compressed-economy form) — no breakdown, no atmospheric intro, no quiet bridge. Drop happens at bar 1 and never releases until track end. Choruses/peaks can ADD density (more layers, more saturation) but never REDUCE intensity.
